serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Dahi Puri is approximately 150 g, typically comprising 4–6 filled puris topped with yogurt, chutneys, and sev garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Dahi Puri as a composite dish. Values are estimated by component-level aggregation using ICMR-NIN data for semolina puri, boiled potato, chickpea, plain yogurt, tamarind chutney, and sev, consistent with the input record values. Input confidence 0.9 retained. Saturated fat estimated at 2.5 g/100 g based on yogurt and frying oil contribution; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and moisture breakdown is not available in ICMR-NIN for this composite preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dahi Puri as a composite dish. GI of 55 (low) is estimated by analogy with mixed Indian chaat dishes containing refined flour (semolina puri, GI ~65–70), boiled potato (GI ~78), and yogurt (GI ~36), moderated by protein and fat content from yogurt and sev. The blended GI estimate of 55 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed Indian snacks. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 27.75) / 100 ≈ 15.3 g (carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 18.5 × 1.5 = 27.75 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg/100 g; ~525 mg per 150 g serving) from sev, chaat masala, and chutneys
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Dahi Puri to occasional small portions and request reduced salt and chaat masala, as a single 150 g serving provides approximately 525 mg sodium — over 20% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (55) with moderate glycemic load (~15 per serving) from refined semolina puris, boiled potatoes, and sweetened yogurt
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Dahi Puri due to its refined carbohydrate base and added sugars in sweetened yogurt and tamarind chutney; if consumed, portion should not exceed 75 g and unsweetened yogurt should be requested.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.5 g/100 g) from deep-fried puris and yogurt, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Dahi Puri sparingly, as the combination of deep-fried puris, saturated fat from yogurt, and high sodium content may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; baked or air-fried puri variants with low-fat yogurt are preferable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dairy and deep-fried components may trigger inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Yogurt provides probiotics that may benefit gut health and some inflammatory markers, but deep-fried puris and dairy can act as inflammatory triggers in certain individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; monitor individual tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dahi Puri is a beloved Indian chaat originating from Maharashtra, particularly associated with Mumbai street food culture. The dish consists of small, hollow, crispy puris made from semolina or refined flour that are deep-fried until golden and puffed. At the point of serving, each puri is gently cracked open and filled with a mixture of mashed boiled potatoes and boiled chickpeas, then generously topped with thick sweetened yogurt (dahi). The assembled puris are finished with drizzles of tangy tamarind chutney and spicy green chutney made from coriander, mint, and green chilli, followed by a heavy garnish of fine sev (crispy chickpea-flour noodles), pomegranate seeds, and fresh coriander leaves. The result is a multi-textural, multi-flavour experience combining creamy, crunchy, sweet, sour, and spicy sensations in a single bite. Nutritionally, Dahi Puri provides approximately 160 kcal per 100 g, with 4.5 g protein, 7.5 g fat, and 18.5 g carbohydrate. The yogurt contributes calcium (85 mg/100 g) and vitamin B12 (0.3 mcg/100 g), while chickpeas and potatoes provide dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g) and folate (35 mcg/100 g). Sodium is relatively high at 350 mg/100 g due to sev and spice mixes. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten and dairy. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without significant modification. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 15 per standard 150 g serving.

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are estimated by component-level aggregation using ICMR-NIN data for constituent ingredients of Dahi Puri; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite chaat dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparable entries. Saturated fat is estimated. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile and moisture data for Dahi Puri as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. saturated_fat_g (2.5 g/100 g) is estimated from the yogurt and deep-frying oil contribution and is not directly reported in an approved source for this composite preparation. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dahi Puri exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The portions array in the input contained three identical duplicate entries (150 g); deduplicated to a single 150 g serving. 5. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 6. This dish is Indian (not Singaporean), so Chinese, Malay, and Tamil local names are not mandatory; Hindi local name is provided as the primary regional language.
